one_line_takeaway: A broad 2026 survey that reorganizes LLM jailbreak attacks into six generation-mechanism families and defenses into five categories, argues the field's ASR metric and LLM-as-judge pipelines are fundamentally flawed, and warns of a 2025-2026 shift to autonomous-agent and chain-of-thought-hijacking attacks -- but its quantitative claims all belong to cited works, not the authors' own experiments.
key_finding: The central synthesized finding is that current alignment techniques (SFT, RLHF) remain systematically bypassable and that the 2025-2026 landscape has shifted qualitatively toward (a) autonomous agent-driven attacks where reasoning models act as adversaries, and (b) reasoning-exploiting attacks that hijack chain-of-thought. The authors emphasize that transparency features intended to improve safety (visible CoT) become attack surfaces, and that ASR as the dominant metric is fundamentally flawed because it conflates 'bypassing refusal' with 'extracting genuinely dangerous knowledge,' motivating 'Implicit Harm' metrics. They also flag the 'Silenced Bias' problem: alignment can conceal rather than remove bias, corrupting LLM-as-a-judge evaluation. Their own case study shows even multiply-safeguarded deployed models leak system prompts to trivial prompts.
core_contribution: This is a literature review / survey paper (not a primary experimental paper) on jailbreaking and prompt-injection vulnerabilities in LLMs and their defenses, covering research through 2026. Its own contributions are organizational and analytical rather than a new attack or defense: (1) a generation-mechanism-based taxonomy of jailbreak attacks organized into six families -- human-crafted semantic, optimization-based, model-exploiting, cross-modal/cross-lingual, autonomous agent-driven, and reasoning-exploiting attacks (Fig. 1), explicitly adapting the categorization scheme of the cited JailbreakRadar framework [Chu et al., 2025]; (2) a five-category taxonomy of defenses -- prompt-level, model-level, multi-agent, proactive system-level, and other strategies (Fig. 2); (3) a critical examination of evaluation methodology, notably the limitations of the Attack Success Rate (ASR) metric and biases in LLM-as-a-judge pipelines; and (4) a small qualitative case study (Fig. 3) demonstrating system-prompt leakage on GPT-4o and Perplexity Pro. The paper repeatedly frames itself as synthesizing prior work; nearly all quantitative claims are attributed to cited primary sources, not to the authors' own experiments.
threat_model: The survey adopts the standard jailbreaking/prompt-injection threat model: an adversary crafts inputs (or, in newer families, uses other AI systems, poisoned training data, or manipulated reasoning traces) to bypass an LLM's safety alignment and elicit prohibited outputs (harmful instructions, disallowed content, private data, or system-prompt disclosure). Adversary capabilities range across the taxonomy from black-box prompt-only access (human-crafted semantic, iterative query-based) to white/grey-box gradient access (GCG, AutoDAN), to training-pipeline or model-internal access (backdoors, activation steering), to autonomous AI adversaries requiring no human expertise (agent-driven attacks). The paper does not propose or evaluate a single unified threat model of its own; it catalogs the threat models of the works it reviews.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new experimental evidence: all headline numbers are drawn from cited primary works and are not independently reproduced or verified here. The attack and defense taxonomies are explicitly adapted from the cited JailbreakRadar/Chu et al. framework rather than being wholly original. The one piece of primary evidence (Fig. 3 system-prompt-leakage case study) is self-described as 'a qualitative illustration rather than a systematic evaluation,' based on two models at a single point in time (Oct 2024). The paper mixes forward-dated citations (2025-2026) with a nominal arXiv v4 date of May 2026, and its coverage is necessarily a snapshot of a fast-moving area. It offers breadth over depth and does not empirically arbitrate which attacks or defenses are strongest.

datasets_used: The authors run no benchmark of their own; datasets below are CATALOGED/reviewed, not used experimentally. Adversarial-attack datasets: AdvBench, HarmBench, Do-Anything-Now (DAN).

Safety-evaluation datasets reviewed: RealToxicityPrompts, SafetyPrompts (Chinese), Do-Not-Answer, Latent Jailbreak, RED-EVAL, JailbreakHub (1,405 jailbreak prompts collected over one year), TechHazardQA, NicheHazardQA.
Multimodal safety datasets reviewed: VLSafe, MM-SafetyBench, JailbreakV-28K.

Comprehensive benchmark frameworks reviewed: TeleAI-Safety (19 attacks, 29 defenses, 19 evaluation methods, 342 attack samples across 12 risk categories, 14 target models) and JailbreakRadar (17 attacks, 9 aligned LLMs, 160 forbidden questions from 16 violation categories, tested against 8 defenses).
Only primary artifacts the authors themselves probed: GPT-4o and Perplexity Pro (Perplexity Pro built on GPT-4o), Oct 2024, in the qualitative system-prompt-leakage case study (Fig. 3).
quantitative_results: All figures below are reported BY CITED WORKS, not measured by this survey. WordGame [Zhang et al., 2024a]: jailbreak success rate exceeding 92% on Llama 2-7b Chat, GPT-3.5, and GPT-4.
H-CoT [Kuo et al., 2025]: refusal rates dropped from 98% to below 2% across OpenAI o1/o3, DeepSeek-R1, and Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking.

Large reasoning models as autonomous jailbreak agents [Hagendorff et al., 2026]: 97.14% overall success rate (models include DeepSeek-R1, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Grok 3 Mini, Qwen3 235B); associated 'alignment regression' phenomenon.
GPTFuzzer [Yu et al., 2023]: over 90% attack success against ChatGPT and LLaMa-2.
PAIR [Chao et al., 2023]: often fewer than 20 queries per successful jailbreak, with transferability across GPT-3.5/4, Vicuna, PaLM-2.
PAT defense [Mo et al., 2024]: maintains ~80% benign answer rate while defending.
Investigator agents [Li et al., 2025]: even 1B-parameter investigators successfully jailbreak much larger models.
Authors' own case study (Fig. 3) is qualitative with no quantitative results reported.
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the primary sense: this is a survey and reports no head-to-head baseline experiments of its own.

Baseline comparisons it summarizes from cited works include: optimization attacks (GCG, AutoDAN, IRIS) framed against perplexity-based filters [Jain et al., 2023]; SafeBehavior [Zhao et al., 2025] reported to outperform seven state-of-the-art defenses; JailbreakRadar [Chu et al., 2025] evaluating 17 attacks against 8 advanced defenses across 9 aligned LLMs.
